From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Village and civil parish in Northern England

This article is about a village. For a larger settlement nearby which developed through coal mining, see [Easington Colliery](/wiki/Easington_Colliery "Easington Colliery").

**Easington**, also known as **Easington Village**, is a village and [civil parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") in eastern [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham"), England. It is located at the junction of the [A182](/wiki/A182_road "A182 road") and B1283, leading north-west to [Hetton-le-Hole](/wiki/Hetton-le-Hole "Hetton-le-Hole") and south east to [Horden](/wiki/Horden "Horden").

It is near the [A19](/wiki/A19_road "A19 road"), which travels north to [Seaham](/wiki/Seaham "Seaham") and [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland") as well as south to [Peterlee](/wiki/Peterlee "Peterlee") and [Stockton-on-Tees](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees"). The population of Easington Village was 2,164 in 2001,[[1]](#cite_note-1) increasing slightly to 2,171 at the 2011 Census.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Easington,_County_Durham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/St_Mary_the_Virgin%2C_Parish_Church_of_Easington_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1706454.jpg/250px-St_Mary_the_Virgin%2C_Parish_Church_of_Easington_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1706454.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary_the_Virgin,_Parish_Church_of_Easington_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1706454.jpg)

St Mary the Virgin, Easington

There is evidence of Easington having been an important pre-[Norman Conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest") site, including architectural fragments (dating from as early as the 8th century) found within the fabric of St Mary's Church. St Mary's itself is mostly 12th–13th century, and contains a notable amount of seventeenth-century woodwork.[[3]](#cite_note-3) From 1256 until 1832 the [Rector](/wiki/Rector_(ecclesiastical) "Rector (ecclesiastical)") of Easington was also [Archdeacon of Durham](/wiki/Archdeacon_of_Durham "Archdeacon of Durham").

One of the most prominent events in the long history of the village was the hanging of two men on the [village green](/wiki/Village_green "Village green") for involvement in the plot to replace Tudor monarch [Queen Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") with [Mary, Queen of Scots](/wiki/Mary,_Queen_of_Scots "Mary, Queen of Scots"). [Pope Adrian IV](/wiki/Pope_Adrian_IV "Pope Adrian IV") (c. 1100–1 September 1159), born [Nicholas Breakspear](/wiki/Nicholas_Breakspear "Nicholas Breakspear"), lived here for a time.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The village is also known as the setting of the folktale, "The Legend of the Easington hare".[[5]](#cite_note-5)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Seaton_Holme%2C_Easington_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1706399.jpg/250px-Seaton_Holme%2C_Easington_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1706399.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seaton_Holme,_Easington_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1706399.jpg)

Seaton Holme

The village is home to one of the few remaining 13th-century domestic buildings (open-hall) in the country, Seaton Holme. It became an archdeacon's residence, served as the rectory until around 1960 and was a children's home for a time before falling into disrepair. In 1992 it was finally restored to a semblance of its former stature.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

From 1894 to 1974 Easington was in [Easington Rural District](/wiki/Easington_Rural_District "Easington Rural District"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) in 1974 Easington became part of [Easington](/wiki/Easington_District "Easington District") non-metropolitan district. On 1 April 1983 the parish was abolished and split to form [Easington Colliery](/wiki/Easington_Colliery "Easington Colliery") and "Easington Village".[[8]](#cite_note-8) At the 1971 census (one of the last before the abolition of the parish), Easington had a population of 9264.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 2009 Easington became part of [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham_(district) "County Durham (district)") unitary authority area.

### Overshadowed

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Easington,_County_Durham&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Overshadowed")]

The sinking of [coal mines](/wiki/Coal_mines "Coal mines") near the village began on 11 April 1899. The settlement of [Easington Colliery](/wiki/Easington_Colliery "Easington Colliery") developed around the colliery. The settlements along the B1283 road has resulted in both settlements merging. However, the two places have retained their distinctive characters and continue to reflect different trends. Easington Colliery was the last pit to close on the [Durham Coalfields](/wiki/Great_Northern_Coalfield "Great Northern Coalfield") in 1993, with the loss of 1,400 jobs.

## Amenities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Easington,_County_Durham&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Amenities")]

There were two post offices in Easington. The one in the town serves the top of Easington, the middle post office serves the area which is predominantly council properties, and the lower post office served the colliery housing area. This post office closed on 10 October 2008 after being cut in the closure scheme by the Post Office.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Easington Academy](/wiki/Easington_Academy "Easington Academy") is located in the village. It acts as the main [secondary school](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school") for the village and surrounding area.

## Demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Easington,_County_Durham&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Demography")]

Easington is notable for being the town with the highest percentage of white residents in England (99.2% white in 2001).[[10]](#cite_note-10) According to the results of the [2001 census](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_census "2001 United Kingdom census"), it also has the UK's lowest population of [Jedi knights](/wiki/Jedi_census_phenomenon "Jedi census phenomenon").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Notable people

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Easington,_County_Durham&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Notable people")]

* [Matt Baker](/wiki/Matt_Baker_(presenter) "Matt Baker (presenter)") – television presenter (*[Blue Peter](/wiki/Blue_Peter "Blue Peter")*, *[Countryfile](/wiki/Countryfile "Countryfile")*, *[The One Show](/wiki/The_One_Show "The One Show")*)
* [Dennis Donnini](/wiki/Dennis_Donnini "Dennis Donnini") – [VC](/wiki/Victoria_Cross "Victoria Cross") recipient
* [Steve Harper](/wiki/Steve_Harper_(footballer) "Steve Harper (footballer)") – [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C.") goalkeeper
* [Rachel Howard](/wiki/Rachel_Howard "Rachel Howard") – artist
* [Jez Lowe](/wiki/Jez_Lowe "Jez Lowe") – folksinger and songwriter
* [Kevin Scott](/wiki/Kevin_Scott_(footballer) "Kevin Scott (footballer)") – Newcastle United footballer
* [Tom Simpson](/wiki/Tom_Simpson "Tom Simpson") – champion cyclist
* [Adam Johnson](/wiki/Adam_Johnson_(footballer) "Adam Johnson (footballer)") – Sunderland footballer
* [Alan Tate](/wiki/Alan_Tate_(footballer) "Alan Tate (footballer)") – [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City_A.F.C. "Swansea City A.F.C.") footballer
* [James Isaacson](/wiki/James_Isaacson "James Isaacson") – Newcastle Falcons rugby player
* [Ian Cranson](/wiki/Ian_Cranson "Ian Cranson") – Ipswich, Sheffield Wednesday and Stoke City footballer.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Easington,_County_Durham&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The film *[Billy Elliot](/wiki/Billy_Elliot "Billy Elliot")*, set in the fictional [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") town of Everington, was mainly shot in Easington, though the filmmakers had to go a long way north to [Ellington](/wiki/Ellington,_Northumberland "Ellington, Northumberland") to find the only working mine in the North East. The subsequent stage musical version specifically identifies Easington as its location.

Easington was one of the locations the [Ken Loach](/wiki/Ken_Loach "Ken Loach") directed film [The Old Oak](/wiki/The_Old_Oak "The Old Oak") was filmed.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
